medications: Famotidine 20 mg as needed at night
Melatonin 3 mg as needed for sleep
Loratadine 10 mg daily during allergy season
health.mental_health: Since the promotion into management, she’s been dealing with persistent anxiety, irritability, and trouble sleeping. She’s embarrassed about feeling out of her depth and has had a few near-panic moments before leading meetings; she’s considering therapy through her employee assistance program but worries it could affect how she’s perceived at work.
recent_visits: Primary care visit (late January) for worsening nighttime reflux and elevated blood pressure; discussed diet changes and stress management
Occupational health check-in (early February) after a minor back strain on site; cleared for full duty with stretching and lifting guidance
Dental cleaning (December); noted enamel wear possibly worsened by reflux

finance.debt: Car loan with about $11,000 remaining and roughly $6,500 on credit cards that crept up during the last few months (work boots/tools, unexpected home repairs, and eating out more due to long hours). She also has a medical payment plan for a prior endoscopy and follow-up testing related to GERD.
investments: Emergency savings in a high-yield savings account
recent_transactions: Paid $245 for a clinic visit and lab work copay/deductible related to reflux and blood pressure follow-up
Purchased $189 steel-toe boots and new PPE after moving into more site-walk and inspection duties
Paid $1,480 for a furnace repair during a cold snap
Transferred $300 to a savings account after first paycheck reflecting the promotion

relationships: Spouse: Mark Fischer (works in IT support; supportive but frustrated by her unpredictable hours)
Older sister: Adaeze Okafor (lives in Chicago; they talk weekly, especially when Ngozi vents about management pressure)
Mentor: Denise Carlson (retired superintendent; occasional phone check-ins and advice)
Crew lead: Luis Ramirez (former peer now reporting to her; relationship has become tense since the promotion)
Close friend: Janelle Brooks (neighbor; walks together when schedules align)
social.social_media_activity: Mostly a quiet Facebook presence—family photos, weather complaints, and the occasional post about jobsite safety or women in trades. She avoids posting about work conflicts but has liked posts about burnout and leadership lately.
private_conversations: Confided to her sister that she’s worried she was promoted because the company needed a ‘diversity win’ and now feels she has to be perfect
Told her spouse she’s considering looking for another job because she dreads managing people more than the physical work
Discussed with her mentor a recent incident where she froze during a safety briefing and felt humiliated
Vented to a friend about a crew member undermining her authority and her fear of being labeled ‘too emotional’ if she escalates it

schedule.work_hours: Typically Monday–Friday 6:00 a.m.–4:30 p.m., with rotating early-morning site walks and occasional Saturdays (especially during pours/inspections). Management duties add 1–2 hours of paperwork and emails most evenings.
regular_appointments: Monthly safety committee meeting (first Tuesday, 3:30 p.m.)
Biweekly check-in with project manager (every other Thursday, 2:00 p.m.)
Quarterly primary care follow-up for blood pressure/reflux tracking
Weekly grocery run Sunday mornings to keep reflux-friendly foods stocked
upcoming_events: City inspection walk-through for a multifamily renovation (next Wednesday, 9:00 a.m.)
Union training session on documentation and incident reporting (two weeks out, Friday afternoon)
Family dinner for her sister’s visit (next month, Saturday evening)
schedule.daily_routine: Up at 4:45 a.m., coffee (often despite reflux), quick breakfast if she can tolerate it, on site by 6:00. She tries to eat smaller lunches but often ends up grabbing something quick between subcontractor coordination and inspections. Evenings are split between dinner, catching up on logs/emails, and trying to wind down; sleep is frequently interrupted by stress or heartburn.

professional.company: North Loop BuildWorks, a mid-sized commercial general contractor based in Minneapolis
projects: Warehouse-to-office conversion in the Northeast neighborhood (tenant improvements and MEP coordination)
Multifamily renovation near Uptown (phased work with tight inspection windows)
Small public-works sidewalk and ADA ramp package (coordination with city inspectors and traffic control)
professional.performance_issues: Since moving into management responsibilities, she’s struggling with documentation, delegation, and conflict management. She’s missed a couple of end-of-day reports, feels slow in scheduling subs, and is second-guessing decisions in meetings. She worries her stress is showing and that one mistake could lead to a safety incident or a budget overrun.
workplace_relationships: Project manager (Evan Hultgren) expects faster reporting and has been blunt in emails, which increases her anxiety
Superintendent (Tara Nguyen) is supportive but has limited time; pushes Ngozi to ‘lead with authority’
A few crew members who were peers now test boundaries, especially around task assignments and breaks
HR contact (Megan Schultz) suggested leadership coaching; Ngozi is hesitant to engage formally

hobbies: Weekend walks around Lake Nokomis when her reflux and sleep allow
DIY home improvement projects (painting, minor carpentry) to feel competent outside of work
Listening to true-crime and construction podcasts during commutes
Indoor plants and small herb garden by the kitchen window
habits: Drinks 2–3 cups of coffee most mornings even though it worsens GERD
Stress-eats late at night when catching up on emails, which triggers heartburn
Keeps antacids in her truck and bedside drawer
Has a couple of drinks on some weekends to ‘shut her brain off’ after rough weeks (she’s aware it can worsen reflux and sleep)
Tries to elevate the head of her bed and avoid spicy foods, but slips when exhausted
lifestyle.living_situation: Lives in a modest single-family home in South Minneapolis with her spouse. The promotion has improved income but also reduced time at home; household chores and meals have become a point of tension, and she’s been paying for more takeout and convenience services than she used to.
lifestyle.dietary_preferences: Attempts a GERD-friendly diet (smaller meals, less tomato, fried food, mint, and late-night eating). She does best with oatmeal, bananas, lean proteins, and simple soups, but long shifts and site food options make consistency hard.
